Goodbye My Friend

Knut Olson - 1922-2000

[Image: Photograph of Knut Olson smiling outdoors. Caption: Knut Olson at the 1999 U.S. O' Championships]

Oh, we never know where life will take us
I know it's just a ride on the wheel
And we never know when death will shake us
And we wonder how it will feel

So goodbye, my friend
I know I'll never see you again
But the time together through all the years
Will take away these tears
It's OK now

Goodbye, my friend

Knut Olson, one of Cascade's long-time members, passed away on October 10. Knut was a fixture in the club, traveling all over the U.S. and the world to participate in orienteering events. He often had to compete against “those young guys” in a younger age category when there was no category for him. But that didn't bother Knut, who usually won anyway.

Knut was originally from Sweden, and orienteered there before moving to the U.S. in 1948. He rediscovered orienteering here in the Seattle area in 1984, and there was no stopping him after that. At his “first” meet at Woodland Park, Knut discovered orienteering had changed quite a bit over the previous 40 years. Accustomed to black-and-white, small-scale maps, the large-scale color map of Woodland Park threw him off so much that he didn't finish the course. But he persisted, and in 1986 won his first of 3 U.S. Championship titles, his third coming last year at Lake Tahoe.

Knut hosted several club social events at the Gold Creek Trout Farm in Woodinville, which he had purchased in 1977 and turned into a successful business.

Knut was well-known all over the country and will be missed by all of us.

WIOL Begins November 4

If you start seeing more school-age children at local orienteering meets, it's not because they've gotten lost on the way to the mall. They're participating in the Washington Interscholastic Orienteering League.

The WIOL begins its 19th season next month with the November 4 meet at Woodland Park. The League was started by Cascade as a way to introduce school-age children to the wonderful sport of orienteering.

For more information about the WIOL meets, see the event schedule at the end of this newsletter. If you'd like a WIOL brochure, call the Hotline (206-783-3866) and we'll mail one to you. Or, visit the Pacific Northwest Orienteering web page at www.pnwo.org.

O' Series Starts 9th Season

The Winter O' Series, which runs concurrently with WIOL, starts its ninth season on November 4 at Woodland Park. To participate, just run the longest course at 4 or more O' Series events. You will receive points based on your finish position, continued ➔

Page 2

with your 4 best results added together to get your O' Series total. The top 3 men and top 3 women will receive awards.

Newsletter

You may have noticed that this issue of Bearing 315 says May-December. Because we haven't had an editor, Bearing 315 was last published in March. Although we still have not found a “permanent” editor, I will be doing the job until I can find a replacement. Ardis Dull has agreed to do printing and mailing, which is a big part of the job.

I think it's important for the club to have a newsletter that actively seeks all our members. Even though we have a web page, the newsletter is an important link to those who either don't have internet access or who prefer a compact, convenient form of news. An additional advantage is that the newsletter is delivered to you, rather than placing the burden on you to seek out our web page. And with the large number of free-time options available to people in this area, we need to do all we can to persuade them to choose orienteering. A big part of that is making information easily available to them.

If you would like to edit the newsletter, please let me know. I am eager to share the glory with others. Contact me at 425-488-3691 or dnewel@gte.net.

Debbie Newell, the Accidental Editor

Adventure Run November 19 at St. Edward Park

Join us for the 2nd annual Adventure Run, a special orienteering-like 10k trail run. You can either follow a marked trail route or use your map to take shortcuts to the markers. Co-hosted by Club Northwest, this event was very well-attended in its debut last year, drawing close to 80 people, most of them runners rather than orienteers. See the schedule for additional information.

Course Designers and Meet Directors Needed

We have an immediate need for meet volunteers in the next few months, particularly course designers and meet directors. Our normal winter schedule means more frequent meets and, hence, a greater need for volunteers to staff them.

If you would like to design courses or direct meets, but don't feel confident, you can assist someone else and learn the ropes.

Please look at the meet schedule and select a meet at which you would like to help. Call the meet director listed for that meet and volunteer to help. If no meet director is listed, call Dave Tallent (425-488-3691) and either volunteer to be meet director, or volunteer for another position. Either way, please help out.

COC Annual Meeting, Dinner, and Election of Officers

Our annual meeting, dinner, and club elections will take place at Coco’s Restaurant in the Lake Forest Park Towne Centre upper level on Sunday, November 12. We will, as always, spend as little time as possible on elections, and as much time as possible eating and socializing. There will also be a club board meeting preceding the annual meeting for those who want to attend.

Mail-in Voting

Each year at our annual meeting, we elect 5 members to the COC Board of Directors. These 5 members then select a President, Vice President, Secretary, and Treasurer. The President then appoints the club coordinators, and the whole bunch makes up the Board of Directors. Any board meeting may be attended by anyone.

If you do not plan to attend the annual meeting, but still want to vote, you may mail or fax the ballot below. All ballots must be RECEIVED by November 10.

Wallula Junction, Wallula, WA — April 8

Sacajawea Orienteers
Meet Director/Course Setter: Ivar Husa
Control Pickup: Frank Skorina, Ivar Husa, Louise K.
Weather: Pretty darn nice. Warm, light breeze, sunny (perfect for the Northern Harrier Hawks who cruised silently close over the sage covered hillsides)

Folks who didn’t take the time to come out for this event missed a good one. Unusual challenges were cast before the course setter. A visit to the site a week before the event (always a good idea) revealed that areas had been flooded (artificially) and others burned. Some adjustments were made to the control locations and courses were set. The morning of the event revealed more extensive burning and flooding, requiring the elimination of one control and the relocation of another.

Page 4

This event marked the first time we’d set an advanced course to use “memory” in lieu of a map in hand. For those not familiar, competitors are shown the map with the start and first control plotted. They then set off for that location ‘from memory’. Need to jog the memory? Gotta go back to the last control. Each of the first six controls had maps showing the current location, and the next control placement. After control 6, a regular map was issued.

In hindsight, maybe we should of just not given them maps. One third of our Advanced Course competitors were caught looking at their maps while running and tripped and fell onto a dusty, sooty, surface that had been recently burned. (No way he was getting into MY car.)
Ivar and Susan Husa

Washington Park, Anacortes - April 16

Chuckanut Orienteers
Course setting and Meet Director: Bill Green
Start/Finish: Ken Klepsch
Registration: Johanne Klepsch
Control placement and pickup: Bill, Ken, Thomas Opryszek, Johanne
Refreshments: Johanne
Beginner Instruction: Terradan Landchild Sagewynd

The weather forecast for the day happily turned out to be wrong, with a mostly sunny and fairly cool day intead of an overcast showery one. The turnout was modest, just a little more than last year.

My aim as a course setter here is to set courses that are interesting, that sample some of the many beauty spots of this park, and that avoid the ”darker green” areas and the hazardous slopes on the south side. For course 4, I particularly wanted to avoid too much thoughtless running. A few controls were hidden, and a few were on unmapped features. These were compromises I made to make the legs a little more challenging, without, I hope, making them unfair. I also had doglegs, crossing legs and nearby controls, but then this isn’t an A meet. The reaction varied from “fantastic” to an unqualified ”atrocious.” The latter is a first for me, and made me wonder, for a while, exactly why I was spending my time doing this. Rick won on course 4 with a time of about 80 minutes, just like he did last year.

Johanne’s soup was great, especially during the chilly wait for the course 4 runners to come back.

Many thanks to everyone for coming out, and to the regular crew for helping out.

Although we did not market it as such, this was Official National Orienteering Day 2000, with most clubs around the nation holding meets this weekend. Our meet brought the McAlary family two hours from Asotin, WA along with three of Katy’s 6th grade friends. Member Jean Meyer came to his first meet in over a year and instead of zipping through the advanced course, he sauntered around the beginner’s course with his 5 year old grandson and his 14 week old puppy. We had a few newcomers (Deena Walker, Wayne Kaaen, and Mike Holman) on the advanced course (which would not have won any prizes for difficulty). And Susan Eberlein, wife of founder and former club President Elis Eberlein is back in the area. Husband and kids will follow this summer.

Chamna Nature Preserve, Richland - June 3

Sacajawea Orienteers
Course Setter- Bob Gruel
Event Host and Control Pickup- Michel Billaux

On Saturday in Richland the weather was perfect for hiking, maybe a little too hot for running. Control 2 of the intermediate course was misplaced. The competitors who didn’t find it have been requalified.

What amazed me, is that some participants found it. I didn’t know where it was located. When I picked up the control markers I ran to control 1. Then I continued to control 2. I found the ideal attack point where the trail penetrates into the trees, but the marker was not there. For ten minutes I looked at similar locations (fisherman places). It was not there either. I picked control 3 (and 4) of the advanced course, hoping to see it from the other side of the arm of the Yakima. Without success! Since half the people found it, I concluded that it was visible from an important trail. I came back and found it rapidly.

Sacajawea State Park, Pasco - October 14/15

Sacajawea Orienteers
Meet Director and Course Setter: Ivar Husa
Control Pickup: Ivar, Frank Skorina, and Louise Kite.

Once again, a fine event was held at Sacajawea Park, near Pasco, WA. The weather was gorgeous, as usual. This year’s event was notable for us in that it drew four competitors from the Seattle area. Sacajawea Orienteers' two-day events have to date been simply “same course both days”. This was meant to attract local people with commitments elsewhere on one of these days- but not both.

An email from Rick Breseman (COC) suggested that the event would be more interesting for cross-state travelers if two advanced courses were set. I heartily agreed, and set the two courses. This format will be repeated in the future, to give people with a distance to drive more to look forward to when they get here. We have some fine maps, and they will look fresh to most of you out there! Lots of off-trail navigation is demanded. Very subtle terrain to work with. Generally small maps, but challenging.

The competition was quite good on the advanced courses. The local runners did well against the Carpetbaggers from the Seattle area with Frank Skorina turning in the lowest combined time. That is, until you factor in the fact that the Seattle crew ran both courses (10.6km total) in the same afternoon! These guys are tough!

A great time was had by all.

Meet Director: Kean Williams
Course Designer: Don Atkinson
Marker Setting: Dave Petty

Mapping Update

We now have 2 freshly-updated maps, thanks to a trio of mappers from the Czech Republic. Milan Jirasek, Ondrej Kotecky, and Marketa Jakoubova spent a good part of the summer and fall working on field-checking maps in the Northwest. As a result, we now have up-to-date maps of Farrel-McWhirter Park in Redmond, and Fort Steilacoom Park in Tacoma (to be used for the WIOL and O' Series Championships in February 2001). Pt. Defiance was on our list as well, but was deemed too big a job for the time available.

OCAD Anyone?

We have a few maps we'd like to convert to OCAD. If you are interested in some on-the-job OCAD training, please let me know. The maps on our list right now are Pt. Defiance Park (Tacoma), Cle Elum Ridge (Cle Elum), and Union Bay (Seattle).

The Club will provide a copy of OCAD 7 which you can use to draw the maps. We also have several club members who can give advice and instruction.

If you are interested, let me know.

Debbie Newell, 425-488-3691, dnewel@gte.net

Oh, we never know where life will take us I know it's just a ride on the wheel And we never know when death will shake us And we wonder how it will feel
So goodbye, my friend I know I'll never see you again But the time together through all the years Will take away these tears It's OK now

Knut Olson, one of Cascade's long-time members, passed away on October 10. Knut was a fixture in the club, traveling all over the U.S. and the world to participate in orienteering events. He often had to compete against “those young guys” in a younger age category when there was no category for him. But that didn't bother Knut, who usually won anyway.

Knut was originally from Sweden, and orienteered there before moving to the U.S. in 1948. He rediscovered orienteering here in the Seattle area in 1984, and there was no stopping him after that. At his “first” meet at Woodland Park, Knut discovered orienteering had changed quite a bit over the previous 40 years. Accustomed to black-and-white, small-scale maps, the large-scale color map of Woodland Park threw him off so much that he didn't finish the course. But he persisted, and in 1986 won his first of 3 U.S. Championship titles, his third coming last year at Lake Tahoe.
Knut hosted several club social events at the Gold Creek Trout Farm in Woodinville, which he had purchased in 1977 and turned into a successful business.
Knut was well-known all over the country and will be missed by all of us.

If you start seeing more school-age children at local orienteering meets, it's not because they've gotten lost on the way to the mall. They're participating in the Washington Interscholastic Orienteering League.

The WIOL begins its 19th season next month with the November 4 meet at Woodland Park. The League was started by Cascade as a way to introduce school-age children to the wonderful sport of orienteering.

For more information about the WIOL meets, see the event schedule at the end of this newsletter. If you'd like a WIOL brochure, call the Hotline (206-783-3866) and we'll mail one to you. Or, visit the Pacific Northwest Orienteering web page at www.pnwo.org.

The Winter O' Series, which runs concurrently with WIOL, starts its ninth season on November 4 at Woodland Park. To participate, just run the longest course at 4 or more O' Series events. You will receive points based on your finish position, continued ➔
with your 4 best results added together to get your O' Series total. The top 3 men and top 3 women will receive awards.

You may have noticed that this issue of Bearing 315 says May-December. Because we haven't had an editor, Bearing 315 was last published in March. Although we still have not found a “permanent” editor, I will be doing the job until I can find a replacement. Ardis Dull has agreed to do printing and mailing, which is a big part of the job.

I think it's important for the club to have a newsletter that actively seeks all our members. Even though we have a web page, the newsletter is an important link to those who either don't have internet access or who prefer a compact, convenient form of news. An additional advantage is that the newsletter is delivered to you, rather than placing the burden on you to seek out our web page. And with the large number of free-time options available to people in this area, we need to do all we can to persuade them to choose orienteering. A big part of that is making information easily available to them.
If you would like to edit the newsletter, please let me know. I am eager to share the glory with others. Contact me at 425-488-3691 or dnewel@gte.net.
Adventure Run November 19 at St. Edward Park

Join us for the 2nd annual Adventure Run, a special orienteering-like 10k trail run. You can either follow a marked trail route or use your map to take shortcuts to the markers. Co-hosted by Club Northwest, this event was very well-attended in its debut last year, drawing close to 80 people, most of them runners rather than orienteers. See the schedule for additional information.
Course Designers and Meet Directors Needed

We have an immediate need for meet volunteers in the next few months, particularly course designers and meet directors. Our normal winter schedule means more frequent meets and, hence, a greater need for volunteers to staff them.
If you would like to design courses or direct meets, but don't feel confident, you can assist someone else and learn the ropes.

Please look at the meet schedule and select a meet at which you would like to help. Call the meet director listed for that meet and volunteer to help. If no meet director is listed, call Dave Tallent (425-488-3691) and either volunteer to be meet director, or volunteer for another position. Either way, please help out.
COC Annual Meeting, Dinner, and Election of Officers

Our annual meeting, dinner, and club elections will take place at Coco’s Restaurant in the Lake Forest Park Towne Centre upper level on Sunday, November 12. We will, as always, spend as little time as possible on elections, and as much time as possible eating and socializing. There will also be a club board meeting preceding the annual meeting for those who want to attend.

Each year at our annual meeting, we elect 5 members to the COC Board of Directors. These 5 members then select a President, Vice President, Secretary, and Treasurer. The President then appoints the club coordinators, and the whole bunch makes up the Board of Directors. Any board meeting may be attended by anyone.
If you do not plan to attend the annual meeting, but still want to vote, you may mail or fax the ballot below. All ballots must be RECEIVED by November 10.
The nominating committee is pleased to announce the nominees for the five (5) Elected Members of the COC 2001 Board of Directors.
I certify by my signature below that I am a member in good standing (as of November 10) of the Cascade Orienteering Club.

Place a check in the box next to each candidate you wish to vote for. Vote for a total of 5 individuals (any write-in candidate must be a COC member in good standing as of November 10, 2000 and must have agreed to serve if elected).

Folks who didn’t take the time to come out for this event missed a good one. Unusual challenges were cast before the course setter. A visit to the site a week before the event (always a good idea) revealed that areas had been flooded (artificially) and others burned. Some adjustments were made to the control locations and courses were set. The morning of the event revealed more extensive burning and flooding, requiring the elimination of one control and the relocation of another.

This event marked the first time we’d set an advanced course to use “memory” in lieu of a map in hand. For those not familiar, competitors are shown the map with the start and first control plotted. They then set off for that location ‘from memory’. Need to jog the memory? Gotta go back to the last control. Each of the first six controls had maps showing the current location, and the next control placement. After control 6, a regular map was issued.

In hindsight, maybe we should of just not given them maps. One third of our Advanced Course competitors were caught looking at their maps while running and tripped and fell onto a dusty, sooty, surface that had been recently burned. (No way he was getting into MY car.) Ivar and Susan Husa

Chuckanut Orienteers Course setting and Meet Director: Bill Green Start/Finish: Ken Klepsch Registration: Johanne Klepsch Control placement and pickup: Bill, Ken, Thomas Opryszek, Johanne Refreshments: Johanne Beginner Instruction: Terradan Landchild Sagewynd
The weather forecast for the day happily turned out to be wrong, with a mostly sunny and fairly cool day intead of an overcast showery one. The turnout was modest, just a little more than last year.

My aim as a course setter here is to set courses that are interesting, that sample some of the many beauty spots of this park, and that avoid the ”darker green” areas and the hazardous slopes on the south side. For course 4, I particularly wanted to avoid too much thoughtless running. A few controls were hidden, and a few were on unmapped features. These were compromises I made to make the legs a little more challenging, without, I hope, making them unfair. I also had doglegs, crossing legs and nearby controls, but then this isn’t an A meet. The reaction varied from “fantastic” to an unqualified ”atrocious.” The latter is a first for me, and made me wonder, for a while, exactly why I was spending my time doing this. Rick won on course 4 with a time of about 80 minutes, just like he did last year.
Johanne’s soup was great, especially during the chilly wait for the course 4 runners to come back.
Many thanks to everyone for coming out, and to the regular crew for helping out.
Eric, Travis, Chirs, Jerico, James and Joe

On Saturday in Richland the weather was perfect for hiking, maybe a little too hot for running. Control 2 of the intermediate course was misplaced. The competitors who didn’t find it have been requalified.

What amazed me, is that some participants found it. I didn’t know where it was located. When I picked up the control markers I ran to control 1. Then I continued to control 2. I found the ideal attack point where the trail penetrates into the trees, but the marker was not there. For ten minutes I looked at similar locations (fisherman places). It was not there either. I picked control 3 (and 4) of the advanced course, hoping to see it from the other side of the arm of the Yakima. Without success! Since half the people found it, I concluded that it was visible from an important trail. I came back and found it rapidly.

Once again, a fine event was held at Sacajawea Park, near Pasco, WA. The weather was gorgeous, as usual. This year’s event was notable for us in that it drew four competitors from the Seattle area. Sacajawea Orienteers' two-day events have to date been simply “same course both days”. This was meant to attract local people with commitments elsewhere on one of these days- but not both.

An email from Rick Breseman (COC) suggested that the event would be more interesting for cross-state travelers if two advanced courses were set. I heartily agreed, and set the two courses. This format will be repeated in the future, to give people with a distance to drive more to look forward to when they get here. We have some fine maps, and they will look fresh to most of you out there! Lots of off-trail navigation is demanded. Very subtle terrain to work with. Generally small maps, but challenging.

The competition was quite good on the advanced courses. The local runners did well against the Carpetbaggers from the Seattle area with Frank Skorina turning in the lowest combined time. That is, until you factor in the fact that the Seattle crew ran both courses (10.6km total) in the same afternoon! These guys are tough!
A great time was had by all.
Al, Dan and a Second Al Fiedler

We now have 2 freshly-updated maps, thanks to a trio of mappers from the Czech Republic. Milan Jirasek, Ondrej Kotecky, and Marketa Jakoubova spent a good part of the summer and fall working on field-checking maps in the Northwest. As a result, we now have up-to-date maps of Farrel-McWhirter Park in Redmond, and Fort Steilacoom Park in Tacoma (to be used for the WIOL and O' Series Championships in February 2001). Pt. Defiance was on our list as well, but was deemed too big a job for the time available.

We have a few maps we'd like to convert to OCAD. If you are interested in some on-the-job OCAD training, please let me know. The maps on our list right now are Pt. Defiance Park (Tacoma), Cle Elum Ridge (Cle Elum), and Union Bay (Seattle).
The Club will provide a copy of OCAD 7 which you can use to draw the maps. We also have several club members who can give advice and instruction.
If you are interested, let me know.
